files.name: v003_en_agreed.md
files.text:
**Planparser**

This was a research and development project at my work about object detection on architectural drawings. In essence, it was a proof of concept: I wanted to understand how quickly this task could be brought to a working result, what model quality I could get, and how much data was needed for that, using open datasets with common architectural elements such as doors, windows, furniture, and sanitary elements.

I wanted this project to be useful in practice, so an important part of it was generating a specification of the detected elements. Usually this work is done manually, and that takes a lot of time and has a high risk of error.

My task was to check the whole pipeline end to end: how quickly a model could be trained for 15 classes, what level of quality I could get, and whether it was possible to build a working tool for that.

From the technical side, I trained two detection models: `yolo11l` and `faster r-cnn`. For training, I used floor plan datasets with common architectural elements. Then I compared the models by `P`, `R`, `mAP50`, and `mAP50-95`. The best result was from `yolo11l`: `P=0.901`, `R=0.898`, `mAP50=0.937`, `mAP50-95=0.736`. `faster r-cnn` had a weaker result: `P=0.84`, `R=0.86`, `mAP50=0.727`, `mAP50-95=0.495`. In terms of inference speed, `yolo11l` was about `19.8 ms`, while `faster r-cnn` was slower. The training itself took a few hours of compute on Kaggle, and about one thousand floor plan images were enough to get the result.

After training the models, I built a full application. I made an inference backend with `FastAPI`, a web ui with `Gradio`, and added model selection, bounding box visualization, and configuration through env variables. The application also creates a specification table with the list of detected elements and their counts, and this table can be downloaded in `csv` format. I also packaged everything in `Docker` and published a demo on `Gradio` and `Hugging Face`, so this is a working prototype that you can try on Hugging Face.

This project showed that object detection for drawings can be validated quite quickly without major investment if you use pre-trained models and fine-tune them for the required classes. It also gave a basis for the next step: now we are discussing doing a similar project for a more specific area, such as engineering drawings, but with our own annotated data and internal compute inside the company.

files.name: v003_en_agreed.md
files.text:
# Document Translator App for Word

This project was about translating a large set of existing project documentation for energy stations in the Netherlands. The documentation had to be updated to meet a new regulation: the documents were originally in Dutch, and they had to become bilingual by adding English text. There were two main problems. A regular translation process broke the structure and formatting of Word documents, and it also translated specific technical terms from energy project documentation badly.

The task was to automate the translation of `docx` documents while preserving their structure, formatting, and element layout. The solution had to handle different types of content correctly: regular paragraphs, headings, tables, image captions, formulas, and other blocks. It also had to remove the need for users to mark up documents manually and assemble bilingual versions by hand.

To solve this, I built an application for processing large Word reports and related documents. The first version was a desktop app in Python with a PyQt6 GUI for Windows. The application build was automated through GitHub Actions and packaged with PyInstaller. Later, the project was moved to a web environment and deployed on the company’s internal platform, after which several business units started using it for similar tasks. Since standard Python libraries for working with Word documents were not flexible enough to reproduce complex original formatting without major limitations, I used a more complex `docx -> html -> docx` pipeline through Pandoc. First, the document was broken into elements while preserving formatting, images, and other objects. After that, text blocks were sent to the DeepL API, then the translation was inserted back, and only after that the document was assembled again into Word format.

To improve translation quality, the DeepL integration used extra settings such as language, context, dictionaries, and separate context files. This made it possible to control technical terminology better in project documentation.

The result was that I was able to automate the addition of English translation into Dutch documents without losing the original formatting, while also improving terminology quality through context and dictionaries. It saved weeks of work for project managers and other project participants, and the application started being used inside the company for this type of task.
